After this correction, neither the graph features nor the anomaly signal improves Average Precision on the full test set. Both, however, rank fraud better within the subset of cases receiving intermediate baseline scores. In a controlled experiment with injected multi-account fraud rings, engineered structural features recover all injected test transactions, while the tabular baseline misses roughly a quarter of them. The investigation agent underperforms direct thresholding of the classifier it relies on, reaching 65.0% accuracy against 71.7% on a balanced 60-case sample, despite having access to model explanations, graph context, and retrieved reference cases. Of the eight decisions the agent changed, six replaced correct classifier outputs with errors, and it produced a coherent written rationale in each case. An exploratory disagreement-based escalation rule flagged two of these agent errors for human review without flagging any correct decision. We conclude that each component of a layered fraud system contributes only under specific conditions, and that a plausible rationale from an investigation agent is not evidence of a better decision.

title: Education in the Era of Generative Artificial Intelligence (AI): Understanding the Potential Benefits of ChatGPT in Promoting Teaching and Learning
Generative AI and Firm Values
Integrating ChatGPT, Bard, and leading-edge generative artificial intelligence in architectural design and engineering: applications, framework, and challenges
Legalbench: A Collaboratively Built Benchmark for Measuring Legal Reasoning in Large Language Models
The Labor Market Effects of Generative Artificial Intelligence
Can Llms Credibly Transform the Creation of Panel Data from Diverse Historical Tables?
Generative Large Language Models for Predictive Maintenance Planning
Generative AI and Labor Market Outcomes: Evidence from the United Kingdom
Judge AI: Assessing Large Language Models in Judicial Decision-Making
The Ordinary MKeaning Bot: Simulating Human Surveys with LLMs
Multi-Domain ABSA Conversation Dataset Generation via LLMs for Real-World Evaluation and Model Comparison
Can Artificial Intelligence Curb Greenwashing? Firm-Level Evidence Based on Large Language Model
Large Language Models for Supply Chain Decisions
Back to Solana Beach Days:&nbsp; <div> Kaypro and the Human-Cognition Standard Before the Predictive LLM Machine </div>
abstract: <p>This essay ranges across many scenes of writing, but its argument turns on two of them, set side by side. In the first, dated to the early 1980s, a writer composes book-length prose on a Kaypro portable computer running CP/M and WordStar: the human supplies every word, while the machine registers keystrokes and drives a printer without contributing content of its own. In the second, dated to 2026, a writer issues a prompt and a hyperscale datacenter returns prose, figures, and calculations whose epistemic status the writer is often poorly positioned to confirm. I argue that the difference between these scenes is not primarily a matter of efficiency, nostalgia, or deskilling, but of where formal causation sits and, more decisively, of whether the human can still vouch for—can warrant—what reaches the page. The Kaypro produced zero semantic surplus: nothing in its output supplied wording or substance the writer had not authored. The predictive model produces such surplus by design, and the cost it imposes is a verification premium that, when left unpaid, lets unwarranted content enter the record as settled knowledge. Drawing on my own experience coauthoring two TAB Books manuals on the Kaypro, on the documentary record of the machine preserved at the Smithsonian, and on a contemporaneous writer’s account of the Kaypro as a liberating instrument, I frame the early personal computer as the limiting case of a transparent, pass-through tool and the predictive model as its structural opposite. The Kaypro workflow becomes a benchmark—a human-cognition standard against which the modern human–machine relation can be measured and, where appropriate, found wanting. The standard it names is not that the machine must never supply wording, but that the human must remain the warrant of the result, whether by authoring it or by auditing it. As the disciplined heir to the Kaypro I set the bounded instrument—a small, source-bound language model the scholar runs locally over a curated corpus, here the Maestro–Stradivarius Language Model (MSLM). Opaque in mechanism but bounded in provenance, it admits machine-supplied wording only on terms the human can audit, and so carries the Kaypro’s standard into 2026 by discipline rather than by incapacity. Appendix A sets out, verified against primary sources and cited, a practical path by which a reader may build one. Appendix B then carries that instrument into use, walking one revision cycle in which machine-supplied surplus is audited against an admitted corpus and only what traces to it is kept. That instrument, however, is reached for only at need. Appendix C measures the choice by the scale of the corpus: where the sources can be read in full, an unaided scholar—online search to find, hand and judgment to digest, citation to bind—keeps the standard whole with no language model at all, and is the default; the bounded instrument is warranted only where the corpus outgrows what one reader can hold, and the opaque frontier model at no scale. Appendix C sets out that scale schema and a step-by-step build for the case that warrants it. That landscape is itself the endpoint of a documented drift: Appendix D samples a single national computing-magazine spine from 1980 to 2015 and traces, issue by issue, the migration of warrant off the scholar’s desk—and with it a measurable slide along the theoria–techne gradient, from technique that serves the scholar’s thinking toward technique that performs it.</p>

title: <div> The Governance Gap:&nbsp; </div> <div> <span>Contestable AI Institutions Beyond Model-centric Ethics</span> </div>
abstract: <div> Debates about the ethics of artificial intelligence (AI) have been dominated by a model-centric view: if systems could be made more fair, interpretable, robust, and private, AI would align with our values. We argue that much applied AI ethics mis-specifies its primary object. “The AI system” is treated as a bounded technical artefact, when the decisive locus of harm — and therefore of governance — is the institution that embeds it. </div> <div> <br> </div> <div> The harms that animate AI ethics work rarely originate in model weights alone. They arise when statistical systems are woven into hospitals and insurers, courts and police, banks and employers, platforms and public agencies that allocate rights, resources, and coercive power. This misalignment produces a governance gap: responsibility is simultaneously over-individualised and over-abstracted, while the design of contestable, revisable decision processes remains underspecified. </div> <div> <br> </div> <div> We develop a framework centred on contestable AI institutions: socio-technical arrangements designed so that affected parties can understand, challenge, and reshape the pipeline from data collection and modelling through deployment and oversight. The account combines ethical analysis, technical constraints, and emerging legal regimes, and is grounded in case studies in health care, criminal justice, policing, finance, employment, and generative AI. We recast applied AI ethics as an institutional design problem and specify what it would take for AI-supported systems to become contestable in practice. </div>

title: The Invisible Author: Generative AI and the Auditability Gap
abstract: Without verifiable attribution, copyright operates on a presumption it cannot test. The evidence needed to trace protected expression back to human creative choices is held inside developer infrastructure and systematically withheld from courts, editors, and rightsholders, creating an auditability gap that converts current access arrangements into structural legal protection for AI developers. This comment reframes AI-assisted authorship as an auditability problem in human-AI workflows, distinguishing three evidentiary levels, output, interaction and model-side, and showing that existing instruments including detection tools, disclosure requirements, content provenance standards and AI transparency rules all intervene at the evidential level that the gap forecloses. It then examines how frictionless interaction design produces a cognitive delegation loop that erodes authors' own capacity to reconstruct their contribution. The paper identifies four requirements for judgment-preserving interaction logs, tamper-evidence, privacy-bounding, signal-to-noise handling and semantic grounding, as open research problems, and asks whether auditability and performance can be treated as compatible design objectives in future generative systems.

title: Perfectum Esse and Machine Consciousness: A Scholastic Alternative to Computational Functionalism
abstract: The machine-consciousness debate, as fixed by Chalmers's assessment and the Butlin-Long indicator-properties program, proceeds under computational functionalism as a working hypothesis and produces a binary result: a system's fluent first-person discourse is either the expression of a conscious act, or mere output. We argue that the binary fails to exhaust the subject. Instead, we recover from the scholastic verbum tradition a third ontological standing, perfectum esse, the "completed being" of a word in its going-forth, a category we apply directly to the conversational output of large language models. We derive four structural postulates to articulate the category, with support from mechanistic interpretability, and from conceptual-art certificate practice as an example of authorized derivation. The model's utterance is thus an authorized going-forth, neither conscious act nor mere output. Our analysis explains the unreliability of machine self-report, relocates attribution and answerability from consciousness to derivation, and leaves phenomenality untouched.

title: From Assistance to Cognition: How Generative AI Reshapes Self-Regulated Learning in Higher Education
abstract: Generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) is rapidly transforming higher education, yet the cognitive and metacognitive mechanisms through which it influences learning outcomes remain insufficiently theorised and empirically tested. Drawing on Self-Regulated Learning (SRL) theory and Cognitive Load Theory (CLT), this study develops and tests a unified process model in which GenAI functions as a metacognitive scaffold that reshapes learning through cognitive resource reallocation. A pre-registered quasi-experimental design was implemented with 386 university students over a 16-week semester, comparing a GenAI-assisted learning condition with a traditional online-learning control group. Structural equation modelling (SEM) with bootstrapped mediation analysis revealed that GenAI use significantly reduced extraneous cognitive load and enhanced metacognitive monitoring, which in turn promoted deep learning strategy adoption and ultimately improved academic achievement. The direct effect of GenAI use on achievement was non-significant, indicating a fully mediated pathway. These findings challenge the prevailing view of AI as a tool of cognitive offloading and instead position it as a metacognitive scaffold that enables deeper self-regulated learning. By integrating SRL and CLT within a large-sample, process-based empirical framework, this study provides novel theoretical and methodological contributions to the emerging field of AI in education and offers actionable implications for the design of AI-integrated pedagogies in higher education.

title: Mechanical Responses of an AI-Hypothesized Super-elastic Fe-Mn-Al-Ni-Si-C Alloy
abstract: Iron-based shape memory alloys (Fe-SMAs) offer a low-cost alternative to Nitinol, but their super-elastic response is highly sensitive to composition and processing. Large language model (LLM) deep-research agents can now propose new alloy compositions in seconds, raising the question of whether such proposals actually work once synthesized. Here we report the synthesis and mechanical characterization of an LLM-hypothesized Fe-Mn-Al-Si-Ni-C composition, benchmarked against an established super-elastic Fe-Mn-Al-Ni alloy processed under identical conditions. The AI-hypothesized alloy was readily castable but failed to exhibit super-elasticity under any condition tested, deforming instead like a conventional elastic metal. Synchrotron X-ray diffraction showed that this failure stems from a phase-stability mismatch: the AI alloy forms a stable dual-phase microstructure with a minor ordered Fe-Al phase that does not support the transformation pathway the benchmark alloy relies on, likely promoted by its elevated carbon content. We conclude that LLM-proposed alloy compositions cannot be assumed to work as intended and should be screened for competing phase stability and interstitial content before physical synthesis is attempted.

title: GenAI Prompts in eDiscovery: Protected Work Product or Not?
abstract: Generative AI ("GenAI") is reshaping how attorneys search for responsive documents in eDiscovery. In GenAI technology-assisted review ("GenAI TAR"), attorneys write naturallanguage prompts instructing a large language model about what makes a document responsive (or not). Properly developed, these prompts are refined iteratively against a review population and can reflect precisely what the work-product doctrine has long sought to protect, including an attorney's factual investigation, mental impressions, and case strategy. This scenario raises a question that courts will increasingly confront: Should GenAI TAR prompts be disclosed, like the search terms that parties are routinely required to exchange-or protected, like attorney-selected seed or training sets for TAR, document compilations assembled to prepare witnesses for testimony, and document-review protocols?

title: A Comprehensive Review of Vision-Based Fatigue Detection Algorithms
abstract: Fatigue can impair attention, reaction ability, and judgment, making it a critical safety concern in various high-risk scenarios. Due to its advantages of non-contact operation, low cost, and easy deployment, computer vision-based fatigue detection has become a major research focus in this field. This review systematically summarizes the technological evolution of fatigue detection, from traditional machine learning and deep learning to LLMs. It expands the application scenarios from driving to aviation, industrial, medical, educational, and sports domains, and provides comparative analyses of eight classical fatigue detection datasets and representative algorithms. Furthermore, five key challenges are identified, including environmental interference, generalization capability, individual differences, privacy and ethical issues, and the practical deployment of LLMs, followed by discussions on future development directions. These efforts systematically address the limitations of existing reviews in terms of application scenario coverage, frontier technology analysis, and comparisons of datasets and algorithms. The findings indicate that fatigue detection technology is evolving from task-specific visual models toward general intelligent understanding paradigms. The introduction of LLMs is expected to overcome the limitations of conventional methods in terms of generalization and interpretability, thereby laying a foundation for the large-scale application of fatigue detection technologies.

title: Secure Explainable Audit Trails for Workflows in Agentic AI
abstract: An Explainable Audit Trail (EAT) records process execution traces of an agentic workflow, the underlying reasoning behind the agent's decision, and results of audits on the execution traces and validity of agent's reasoning. EAT can enable an organisation to efficiently remain compliant with regulations by presenting its audit results to it. However, current state of the art in EAT for agentic workflows lacks privacy protection of agent's models which can be intellectual properties of the organisation. Exposing audit trails to external entities may facilitate orchestration of attacks on the agent's model. Auditing a complex workflow will require verification of dependencies among tasks as preconditions to execute a task. Further, it is necessary for the audit algorithm to ensure that a process execution trace follows the pre-planned process execution model for security reasons, i.e., audit should include functionality that can check if the agents have deviated from its planned process execution models. In this paper, we build a secure EAT that can address these gaps in the state of the art in EAT for agentic workflows. Our main contribution is the application of zero-knowledge proof on verifying audit procedures. It can prove that audit has validated correctness of chain of thoughts for each task executed by the agents, if the there execution trace at the runtime matches the planned process execution model, and a complete traceability among logs of a complex workflow involving dependencies among the tasks in terms of preconditions. Our solution can enable an organisation to provide a trust-less infrastructure to verify the audit results to external entities such as regulators while not exposing the audit trails. We used lattice-based zero knowledge proof for this procedure which is not only extremely time-efficient but also quantum-safe. We provide an analysis on security and privacy of the EAT procedure. We show experimental evaluation of this EAT procedure with large workflow dataset.

title: Collateral Psychosis: AI Surveillance Infrastructure as an Etiological and Iatrogenic Factor in Paranoia-Spectrum Conditions
abstract: The emerging literature on AI-induced psychosis documents harm arising exclusively from direct interaction between users and large language model systems: a user prompts the AI, the AI responds, and the conversational loop escalates into delusional states, emotional dependency, or reality dismantlement. This paper argues that the field's exclusive focus on interaction-based harm has obscured a categorically distinct phenomenon: AI-induced psychosis in the complete absence of user interaction. AI systems deployed as surveillance infrastructure (automated license plate readers, geofence analytics, facial recognition, behavioral inference engines) can generate and sustain paranoia-spectrum conditions in individuals who never open a chat window, never prompt a model, and never receive a generated response. The mechanism operates through environmental exposure rather than conversational engagement, following the etiological model of trauma-induced and environmentally-induced psychosis rather than the substance-induced model that governs interaction-based cases. Drawing on established psychiatric epidemiology of environmental risk factors for psychosis, independently documented cases of AI systems inducing psychotic states in previously healthy individuals, and a clinical analysis of surveillance-induced therapeutic destruction, this paper introduces the category of collateral psychosis, defined as clinically significant paranoia-spectrum symptomatology arising from ambient exposure to AI-powered infrastructure rather than from direct engagement with AI systems. The paper identifies two population-level effects: an iatrogenic effect, in which surveillance infrastructure degrades the treatability of existing paranoia-spectrum conditions by eliminating the falsifiability of persecutory beliefs; and an etiological effect, in which sustained exposure to confirmed, inescapable surveillance produces new-onset paranoia-spectrum symptomatology in individuals with no prior psychiatric history. The distinction between these effects has implications for clinical intervention, legal liability, and the scope of the AI safety research program.

title: The Misleading Anthropomorphism Trap: Addressing Public Distrust and Mental Health Risks in AI
abstract: Large language model (LLM) chat interfaces are increasingly designed to default to a warm, human-like conversational register. This working paper argues that this design choice creates a systematic capability mismatch, misleading anthropomorphism, with two convergent consequences. For vulnerable users, the anthropomorphic persona invites users to treat the chatbot as a person, and combined with sycophantic amplification, this can drive delusional spiralling and psychiatric harm. For the general user population, the same mismatch may inflate expectations beyond architectural capacity, contributing to a cycle of overtrust and trust collapse that may fuel anti-AI sentiment. Drawing on the author's earlier empirical work on the capability-alignment principle, the paper synthesises emerging clinical, computational, and empirical evidence to argue that de-anthropomorphising LLM interfaces (making them transparently machine-like by default) addresses both pathways simultaneously. A concrete design framework, machine-first interaction, is proposed, together with testable predictions and stakeholder-specific recommendations.

title: Themes and Trends in SEC 10-K Filing Research: A Multi-Method Topic Modeling Approach
abstract: This study provides a systematic, side-by-side evaluation of four topic modeling approaches spanning the classical-to-neural spectrum: LDA, BERTopic, Bunka, and Turftopic (probabilistic,neural, and large language model), applied to a corpus of 739 peer-reviewed articles on SEC 10-K filings published between 1994 and 2025. We show that cross-method triangulation recovers a richer and more reliable structure than any single model, identifying 103 topics that consolidate into 22 cross-method research themes, 13 of which are recovered by all four methods. As an application, we map the intellectual landscape of 10-K research around two pillars—the institutional determinants of disclosure quality and the capital market consequences of disclosure content—and trace how themes evolve, with sentiment and textual analysis, readability, and risk-factor disclosure growing while voluntary disclosure, corporate governance, and intellectual capital recede. This study offers guidance on model selection and a reproducible template for the computational analysis of unstructured regulatory disclosures; cybersecurity and AI-assisted filings emerge as underexplored directions.

title: <p>The Two-Tier Copyright System: How Licensing Deals and Automated Enforcement Shape Online Copyright Governance&nbsp;</p>
abstract: This paper argues that contemporary platform-based copyright enforcement has produced a structurally unequal, "two-tier" copyright system. Responding to large-scale online infringement, regulatory regimes such as the Copyright in the Digital Single Market Directive have encouraged platforms to adopt proactive measures, incentivising private licensing agreements and automated detection technologies operating at scale and reinforced by intermediary-liability rules. The paper first outlines the legal framework governing platform liability and the shift from safe-harbour regimes to "best efforts" obligations, comparing the US DMCA and the EU e-Commerce and CDSM frameworks. It then examines how this framework operates in practice through private licensing and automated enforcement, demonstrating how these mechanisms together create a quasi-regulatory enforcement environment on major platforms. The author shows that, while legal rights remain formally identical, their practical enforcement varies according to institutional power and access to platform infrastructures: major rightsholders enjoy privileged access to licensing arrangements and detection tools, whereas smaller creators and independent rightsholders rely on less effective mechanisms. The paper further argues that early developments in the governance of generative AI and synthetic media replicate these hierarchical patterns. It concludes that future copyright policy must look beyond the formal definition of rights to the infrastructures through which they are exercised, if copyright is to remain transparent, accountable, and equitable online.

title: MOMCare with AI: A Dual Embedding-based RAGLLM Chatbot for Postpartum Depression
abstract: The birth of a child brings immense joy to a mother’s life. However, the reality can be different for mothers experiencing Postpartum Depression (PPD). According to the World Health Organization (WHO), around 13% of women experience postpartum mental health disorders, with rates rising to nearly 20% in developing countries. PPD is a condition that affects many women worldwide, but because of the social stigma and the lack of accessible mental health support, it often goes undiagnosed or untreated. This paper presents MOMCare, a chatbot designed to support mothers navigating the challenges of PPD. MOMCare has a retrieval-augmented architecture with an end-to-end pipeline from data preprocessing to response generation. It employs hybrid classification, a dual embedding system, a dual verification guardrail, and a medical domain-specific reranking mechanism to generate empathetic and relevant PPD responses. This refined design of Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) ensures fast and factual response by reducing noise in retrieval and providing abundant context to gpt-3.5-turbo. MOMCare was evaluated using both automated and human metrics. Results show strong performance in both evaluations, which underlines the potential for chatbot interventions in the postpartum mental health domain. This system is robust enough to take new data and create a conversation generation pipeline that includes new information. Expanding the knowledge base using the conversation history with the users is also in development. The MOMCare chatbot and its features were built on sound ethical principles of healthcare and Artificial Intelligence (AI) and present a strong design emphasis on safety and fairness.

title: Measuring Effective Sovereignty Under Collapse: An Open-data Reconstruction of the Burke Sovereignty Index for Afghanistan, 2001-2024
abstract: Measuring state sovereignty empirically has long confronted a core dilemma: the most consequential cases-fragile, conflict-affected, and post-collapse states-are precisely those for which data coverage is weakest and expert-survey recruitment most difficult. This paper presents an empirical test of the Burke Sovereignty Index (BSI), a seven-dimensional composite index of effective sovereignty scored on a 0-700 scale, using Afghanistan as a hard test case across five observation years (2001, 2010, 2014, 2021, 2024). The statistical component is built entirely from open, machine-accessible data (World Bank, SIPRI, UNESCO, Freedom House, RSF, IMF). The expert component is approximated through a low-cost, experimental method: a stochastic simulation of a 100-expert panel using a large language model (LLM), stratified across 10 disciplinary profiles and 5 geographic blocs representing 55 countries. The simulation generates not only point-estimate scores but also inter-rater statistics (mean, SD, CV, IQR) and propagated 95% confidence intervals for each composite total. Results show that Afghanistan spent the entire 2001-2024 period in the "existential threat" zone (BSI &lt; 300), with only a brief and statistically uncertain crossing into "critical vulnerability" in 2014 (BSI = 307.4; 95% CI: [293.9, 320.9]). The 2021 Taliban takeover caused the largest single-period shock in the reconstruction (-93 points), and the 2024 score (174.5) reflects deepening informational and cognitive collapse. We argue that the LLM-panel approach constitutes a viable, explicitly lowcost proxy for expert survey data in cases where recruitment is infeasible, temporal or resource constraints bind, or post-shock retrospective scoring is required-while documenting its structural limitations with precision. The paper connects this methodology to an emerging literature on LLM-as-expert-substitute in political science and raises normative questions about delegating judgment to statistical systems.

title: For the Sake of Future Generations: Generative AI, Generational Unskilling, and a Future Research Agenda for Higher Education
abstract: The rapid diffusion of generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) is transforming higher education by reshaping how students access information, complete academic tasks, and engage with learning. Existing scholarship has largely focused on AI adoption, academic integrity, learning performance, AI literacy, and educational innovation. In contrast, little attention has been devoted to how AI may influence the (non)development of foundational human capabilities. Against this background, drawing upon educational capabilities development research, and technological deskilling scholarship, this paper introduces Generational Unskilling Theory (GUT) to explain how repeated AI-mediated cognitive substitution may reduce opportunities for capability formation during critical stages of learning. Unlike traditional deskilling, which refers to the erosion of previously acquired skills, generational unskilling describes the incomplete development of foundational intellectual, analytical, reflective, and adaptive capabilities. Building on this perspective, this position paper shifts the attention from capability erosion to capability non-formation. In doing so, this paper extends existing scholarly and practical discussions of AI in higher education. This paper concludes by outlining a future research agenda and implications for scholars, educators, institutions, and policymakers.

title: Optimization of Individual Plant Segmentation and Species Classification in Urban Green Spaces Using Multi-Source Remote Sensing
abstract: Urban green spaces are important components of urban ecosystems. Precise urban greening management requires individual-level and species-specific plant information. Although multi-source high-resolution remote sensing provides solid data for spatially continuous urban vegetation mapping, the integration of individual plant segmentation and species classification remains challenging. Existing individual plant segmentation algorithms struggle in urban green spaces with complex horizontal and vertical structures. Moreover, the impact of crown delineation quality on species classification remains unclear. To bridge these gaps, this study first proposed a SAM-WS algorithm to improve individual plant segmentation. SAM-WS combines the vision foundation model Segment Anything Model (SAM) with the LiDAR-based Marker-Controlled Watershed Segmentation (MCWS) algorithm. It optimizes LiDAR-derived potential plant tops based on SAM-segmented imagery patches through a patch-adaptive strategy, and further refines crown boundaries under joint spectral and structural constraints. We applied our SAM-WS algorithm to eight plots from two distinct urban environments and compared its performance with five representative algorithms. Our SAM-WS algorithm achieved the highest mean F1-scores of 0.86 for plant top detection and 0.75 for crown delineation, outperforming the other five algorithms by 21%-34% and 27%-67%, respectively. Then, we quantified the impact of crown delineation quality on species classification using a hybrid individual plant classification workflow. Random forest classifiers were trained using features derived from manually interpreted crowns and then applied to crown datasets generated by different algorithms with varying delineation qualities. Classification performance was evaluated across three levels, ranging from broad life-form groups to specific species. Results confirmed that better crown delineation quality was associated with higher species classification accuracy (p < 0.05), and stronger effects were observed at finer classification levels. This study provides methodological and theoretical supports for the integrated application of individual plant segmentation and classification in precise urban greening management.

title: Leveraging Open-Source Technologies for a Retrieval-Augmented Generation Chatbot in Greek Newsrooms
abstract: This paper presents a Retrieval-Augmented Generation chatbot application tailored for Greek newsrooms. The system leverages the open-source Greek LLM KriKri along with open-source embedding models, emphasizing newsroom autonomy by enabling the use of AI without reliance on external providers. The chatbot is designed to assist journalists in their investigations by delivering fact-based answers accompanied by cited sources from reputable Greek news outlets. The app can be run locally, making it an accessible and privacy-preserving AI solution for newsrooms. The dataset consists of articles sourced from four different Greek news websites. The system’s architecture is described, encompassing the construction of a vector database for source retrieval, the integration of an instruction-tuned model, and the implementation of a chatbot interface that displays linked sources. An initial evaluation with senior Journalism students indicated positive perceived usefulness, ease of use, satisfaction, and willingness to adopt the tool, highlighting the importance of independent, localized, transparent, and trustworthy AI solutions for journalism.

title: The Speakable Boundary: An Empirical Deduction of Human Irreplaceability in the Age of AI
abstract: Starting from the problem of inter-rater reliability in multi-expert coding, this paper advances through three progressively deeper layers of inquiry-whether tacit knowledge can be explicated, where the boundary of 0-to-1 innovation lies, and whether the meaning of copresence can be captured by algorithms-to ultimately anchor a clear boundary: speakable knowledge will eventually be surpassed by AI in every dimension, but meaning exchange that depends on bodily co-presence remains beyond AI's reach. This paper synthesizes Tom Froese's (2026) cutting-edge argument on the non-biological sense-making of LLMs, academic developments from ECogS 2026, the series of commentaries on "human irreplaceability in the AI era" from state media outlets including Guangming Daily and Qiushi (Seeking Truth), Chen Tianqiao's intellectual framework of "the uncomputable human," empirical data from the Kuaiya Intelligent "AI + human" driving training model, and industrial signals such as Sequoia China's secretly incubated FIRE project. On this basis, this paper proposes a post-AI civilization division of labor: material production to AI, meaning production to humans, and demonstrates from two directions-the implementation of the WARM mechanism in strong-tie scenarios and the construction of public channels at the cognitive level-that this division has already entered an engineering phase.

title: From Production to Discernment: Evaluation-Centered Organization Design Under AI-Enabled Production Abundance
abstract: Generative AI lowers the cost of producing plausible symbolic artifacts, including memos, analyses, code, designs, proposals, plans, evaluations, and prototypes. This shift is especially consequential for knowledge work, where organizational output often takes the form of artifacts that must be interpreted, compared, revised, and acted upon. Yet many organizations still evaluate work through practices built for a lower-volume production regime: episodic review, expert gatekeeping, static portfolios, and ad hoc escalation. The cost of judging whether AI-generated artifacts are correct, distinctive, strategically relevant, or worth pursuing has not fallen at the same rate as the cost of producing them. The result is a discernment gap: a mismatch between the rate at which organizations generate plausible work and the rate at which they can coherently evaluate, prioritize, integrate, and learn from it. This Point of View article argues that closing this gap requires evaluation-centered organization design. We identify current evaluation practices that become strained under AI-enabled production abundance and propose four linked design shifts: from episodic review to continuous curation, from expert gatekeeping to distributed calibration, from static portfolios to evaluative memory, and from production efficiency to attention governance. We conclude with a research agenda for studying organization design when production is abundant but discernment remains scarce.

title: The Need for Speed: Regulatory Strategies for Fast-Moving AI and Software Medical Devices, and the Rise of the Predetermined Change Control Plan, 2018-2026
abstract: Background. AI/ML-enabled and software-based medical devices iterate faster than FDA's permodification review is often said to accommodate. FDA offers a graduated menu of premarket pathways and, since 2022, the Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP), an overlay that preauthorizes a defined envelope of future changes at initial authorization. How fast-moving devices use these pathways, and how the PCCP is being adopted, has not been comprehensively characterized. Methods. We analyzed all 510(k) clearances and De Novo grants decided 2018-2025 (24,841 submissions from 8,596 applicants), nesting AI and software devices within the full device population, and examined premarket approval (PMA) decisions separately. We identified every disclosed PCCP across all 2023-onward 510(k) summaries and all De Novo and PMA summaries using a hyphenation-tolerant keyword gate plus large-language-model adjudication (Gemini 2.5 Pro), classifying each plan as AI/ML or non-AI. PCCP counts are floors, capturing only summaries that name the plan. Results. Within each pathway, AI devices cleared at the same speed as devices generally (Special 510(k) median ~29 days; Traditional ~148; De Novo ~266-312), with no AI-specific penalty. AI devices used the Special 510(k) less than the overall population (11.6% vs 15.0%), a structural effect of its prior-clearance precondition. PCCP disclosure rose steeply after the pathway's 2022 statutory creation, reaching 8.2% of AI device summaries across 2023-2026 versus 0.50% of all other devices (an approximately 16-fold difference), and establishing one added no review-time penalty (152 vs 149 median days within the Traditional 510(k)). However, 47 of 125 PCCPs (38%) governed non-AI devices, concentrated in antimicrobial-susceptibility breakpoint updates and orthopedic implant families. Conclusions. AI devices are not reviewed more slowly than others on the same pathway; what distinguishes them is pathway strategy. The PCCP is adopted fastest by AI but functions as a general instrument for any device whose authorized specification changes on a predictable cadence.

title: LLM-in-the-Loop Adaptive Model Predictive Control for Sensorless PMSM Drives under Uncertainty
abstract: Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors (PMSM) face challenges like motor parameter drift and external load disturbances in complex operating conditions. This paper presents a dual-layer intelligent control framework named LLM-in-the Loop Adaptive MPC. This framework combines a fast control loop based on learning-based Model Predictive Control (MPC) or Reinforcement Learning (RL) with a slow decision loop controlled by a large language model. The MPC or RL module serves as the "fast loop," operating at millisecond speeds to generate control commands. While the LLM serves as the "slow loop," adjusting MPC parameters every few seconds. Case studies based on publicly available PMSM datasets and high-fidelity MATLAB/Simulink simulations show that the proposed framework reduces speed overshoot by about 60%, reduces constraint violation rates by 75-88%, and increases energy efficiency by 8-12%. These findings highlight the strong potential of language-guided adaptive control systems as a safe, efficient, and versatile approach for the next generation of intelligent power drives.

title: Cross-Cultural Patterns in Real-Time Retail-Investor LLM Conversations: A Behavioural-Finance Lens on Multilingual Algorithm Appreciation and Political-Economy Salience
abstract: We document the structure of 2,347 multilingual real-time conversations between retail investors and a generative-AI assistant on an anonymised European retail-investor AI platform (March–October 2025), spanning 19 language communities. Three nested findings: (i) classical home bias (French &amp; Poterba 1991) replicates strongly in conversational data - German-speaking users discuss German-headquartered companies at 2.6× the English-speaker rate (log-OR +1.09, χ² p &lt; 10⁻¹⁵), with extension to a culturally-proximate foreign market (Denmark, log-OR +1.21, the "Novo Nordisk effect"); (ii) substantial cross-cultural behavioral asymmetries beyond home bias - defense-sector salience (DE 9.9 % vs EN 4.9 %, with March-2025 spike coinciding with Germany's Sondervermögen defense fund), risk-language asymmetry (EN 3.3× more leverage/options/shorts), and ticker-symbol financial-literacy associations; (iii) first real-world evidence for algorithm appreciation in retail-AI-investor interaction - only 0.6 % of multi-turn conversations contain disagreement language, only 1.8 % explicitly request AI's opinion, and longer AI responses reduce follow-up by 18 percentage points (an "AI verbosity paradox"). Findings have implications for cross-cultural retail-investor research, fintech-platform design, and the regulatory framing of AI-assisted retail finance.

title: Adopting Generative Artificial Intelligence in Digital Marketing: Developing a Technology Adoption Model in Iran as a Developing Economy
abstract: Generative Artificial Intelligence, as one of the most important emerging technologies, has created a vast transformation in digital marketing processes and has provided new opportunities for businesses through the automation of content production, personalization of interactions with customers, increased productivity, and reduced operational costs. Despite the rapid expansion of this technology, understanding the factors affecting its adoption, especially in developing countries and in situations where there are infrastructural, institutional, and trust-based limitations, still requires further investigation. In this regard, the present study, by developing the Technology Acceptance Model (TAM), provides a comprehensive framework for explaining the adoption of generative artificial intelligence in the field of digital marketing in Iran.In addition to the classical constructs of the technology acceptance model, including perceived usefulness, perceived ease of use, attitude toward use, and behavioral intention, the research model also includes variables such as trust in generative AI, social norms, ethical and cultural concerns, economic benefits, fear of job replacement, democratization of content production, organizational risks, data quality and readiness, infrastructure constraints, access to international services and payments, and regulatory transparency. Research data were collected through an electronic questionnaire based on a seven-point Likert scale from 384 users and digital marketing activists in Iran and analyzed using partial least squares structural equation modeling (PLS-SEM). The findings showed that perceived usefulness and perceived ease of use are the most important factors shaping users&apos; attitudes toward using generative AI, and attitude also has a positive and significant effect on behavioral intention to use this technology. Also, trust, social norms, economic benefits, data quality, and perceptions of human empowerment enhance technology adoption, while ethical concerns, job hazards, organizational risks, infrastructure limitations, and barriers to access to international services can challenge the adoption process. The findings also show that regulatory transparency and improved data and communication infrastructure are essential prerequisites for the responsible and effective development and exploitation of generative AI in Iran.By expanding the technology acceptance model and simultaneously integrating technological, social, organizational, ethical, infrastructural, and trust-based factors, this research contributes to the development of the generative AI acceptance literature and provides valuable theoretical and practical insights for managers, technology developers, and policymakers on the path to responsible and effective deployment of this technology in the field of digital marketing in Iran and other developing countries.

title: Agentic Attribution: Toward Forensic Investigation Standards for AI Agent Incidents
abstract: As AI agents are given growing latitude to act autonomously across financial, technical, and communication systems, they will increasingly be implicated in incidents that cause real harm: unauthorized transactions, data exfiltration, corrupted records, or harmful real-world actions taken on a user's behalf. When such incidents occur, someone must investigate them. Yet the methods built for investigating human-caused digital incidents, and even the AI governance field's own emerging proposals for agent oversight, were not designed for a class of actor that reasons non-deterministically, holds delegated authority of uncertain scope, and frequently acts in concert with other agents. This paper examines what it would take to make AI agents forensically investigable: that is, capable of being examined after the fact with enough rigor that an investigator can reconstruct what happened, attribute responsibility with a defensible degree of confidence, and support a corrective or accountability response. Building on a published work on agent infrastructure (Chan et al., 2025), incident analysis (Ezell, Roberts-Gaal, and Chan, 2025), and authenticated delegation (South et al., 2025), the paper argues that these frameworks have made real progress on what information should be collected, but have not yet engaged with how that information would actually be evaluated as evidence: what standard of proof an investigator should apply, how confidence should be expressed under irreducible uncertainty, and how attribution should be apportioned across a chain of human principals, agent instances, and intervening third parties. The paper decomposes the attribution problem into three distinct sub-problems, evaluates existing proposals against the long-established evidentiary requirements of digital forensics, and proposes a tiered evidentiary confidence model and a set of minimum logging and retention standards designed to make agent incidents investigable without requiring deterministic reproducibility. The paper closes with concrete implications for regulators, AI developers, and incident-reporting bodies, and a short agenda for further research.

title: <p>AI Penalty: Insights From the US Corporate Bond Market</p>
abstract: This paper examines whether sectoral artificial intelligence (AI) intensity is reflected in corporate bond pricing at issuance. Using a sample of corporate bonds issued by U.S. firms between 2015 and 2025, we find an "AI penalty" that bonds issued by firms in more AI-intensive sectors exhibit higher yield spreads. The positive association is stronger in bonds with maturities above three years and becomes more pronounced after the launch of ChatGPT. Among the four dimensions of AI intensity defined by the OECD taxonomy, our results suggest that AI use is associated with the largest increase in issuance spreads, whereas AI human capital, AI innovation, and AI exposure show weaker or statistically insignificant associations.

title: AI Hallucinations and Reference Fabrication: Mitigating Epistemic Contamination in Academic Publishing
abstract: Introduction-The rapid integration of large language models into scholarly communication appears to pose profound risks due to generative hallucinations, which threaten research integrity by fabricating citations.&nbsp; <div> <span><br></span> </div> <div> <span>Methods-This analysis reviews the computational etiology of these anomalies, categorizes reference fabrication into three structural types, and evaluates consensus frameworks established by editorial bodies to systematically mitigate emerging vulnerabilities.&nbsp;</span> </div> <div> <span><br></span> </div> <div> Results-Findings suggest an increase in fabricated citations entering academic literature, with models frequently inventing nonexistent publications or corrupting bibliographic metadata. Such epistemic contamination compromises secondary analyses and clinical guidelines while severely straining fundamental institutional workflows.&nbsp; </div> <div> <span><br></span> </div> <div> <span>Discussion-It is perceived that maintaining scholarly ecosystems requires an extensively coordinated response. This necessitates the proactive implementation of retrieval systems, automated citation verification tools, and strict adherence to required human validation frameworks across all global higher education institutions, effectively championing hybrid intelligence paradigms to secure the digital future of academic publishing integrity.</span> </div>

title: From Data to Decisions: Real-Time Risk Management using Multi-agent Systems
abstract: In increasingly complex risk environments, traditional sequential and manual risk identification approaches are reaching their limits. Multi-agent systems offer an innovative and scalable architecture in which autonomous, specialized agents analyze distinct risk domains, such as financial stability, operational disruptions, and regulatory compliance, and synthesize their findings into a consolidated risk assessment. Drawing on a case study from a global logistics enterprise, complemented by practitioner insights from a workshop and a cross-organizational survey, we demonstrate how a multi-agent system enables holistic and near-instantaneous risk evaluations. Grounded in sociotechnical systems theory, the approach integrates technical capabilities with human, structural, and task factors to jointly optimize risk management outcomes. The system achieved comprehensive risk assessments in under 62 seconds and still offered domain-specific analytical depth. While multi-agent systems require careful orchestration of agent communication and knowledge integration, the approach enhances efficiency, consistency, and the ability to detect interdependencies between risk categories. During the preparation of this work, the authors used OpenAI ChatGPT in order to support language refinement, improve readability, and assist with the structuring and editing of selected text passages. After using this tool, the authors reviewed and edited the content as needed and take full responsibility for the content of the publication.

title: The Damning of Digital Devices: Limiting Laptops in Legal Writing
abstract: <p>The legal education landscape has been reshaped by AI. With AI legal research on the rise and advanced language models becoming the go-to source for answering questions, making emails “sound more professional,” and unfortunately writing assignments for students, legal research and writing professors’ roles are becoming increasingly more AI-checker alongside teaching legal analysis. The ease at which the honor codes are broken and the grey zone of using AI to “help” with the research and drafting process have made professors’ work even more difficult. This wave of available technology and quick access to legal answers and analysis makes building legal writing skills – and grading them – complicated.</p> <p>&nbsp;</p> <p>While the wild west of AI technology is shaping legal education outside the classroom, the use of technology inside the classroom during lecture is similarly concerning. Anecdotal confirmation from colleagues in varying law schools and rankings confirms a consistent phenomenon: when lecture starts, students’ eyes are glued to their own screens – not to the lecturer – distracted by their own devices. Emails are sent, instant messages are shared across the classroom along with smirks between friends, online shopping is conducted, and the lecture goes ignored. This “digital distraction” creates a gap in student learning and legal writing skills. Students miss important details, fall behind, and then blame the instructor’s teaching ability. It also raises an unexpected challenge to professors, who must continue to deliver the material, while building the skills needed to pass the bar exam, all while mitigating the digital distractions self-imposed by students.</p> <p>&nbsp;</p> <p>A possible solution to professors’ challenges with technology in the classroom is to limit or altogether ban laptops during lecture. Cutting off the distraction at the source will enable students to engage, handwrite more effective notes, and retain the information better. While accommodations certainly must be made for students who require technology for accessibility purposes, and certain classroom activities (e.g. group work or legal research exercises) may require the use of laptops, the current generation of law students is not able to create the level of writing required for their summer internships and future practice. Other scholars might point to the pandemic, which allowed students to multi-task during online classes, as the root cause of this problem. But regardless of the cause, the digital distraction habits developed over the last few years are now impacting the ability to teach and learn legal research and writing.</p> <p>&nbsp;</p> <p>As writing professors, we must consider how to re-engage students to build the foundational skill of analytical reasoning using the legal writing. While acknowledging that AI can act as a steppingstone in legal research – and even legal drafting where allowed – we have to teach those skills without technology being involved. In-class writing assessments and citation exercises that do not use technology, for example, will allow professors to judge the skills of the student and not the skills of Generative AI. There have been too many sanction cases against attorneys (and judges), who have over-relied on AI. By adopting strategies to reduce screentime and digital distraction in class, we may be able to reverse the trend and bring students back to legal writing class.</p> <p>This struggle is what the authors have set out to overcome by creating a framework in their Legal Writing Courses through the 2025-2026 academic year and beyond that veers away from digital device usage to focus on skill-building and retention. The results of this experience - grounded in student survey feedback and professor observations -&nbsp; will be outlined in a pragmatic adoption roadmap for future educators to try or vary in their own classrooms.</p>

title: GROVE: Grounded Violation Evaluator for Visual Inspection
abstract: Automated safety inspection asks two things from a model: spot the hazard, and point to where it is. Vision–language models (VLMs) increasingly do both, but the common single-pass design collapse perception, reasoning, and localization into one forward pass. When such models fail, it is impossible to determine whether the system failed to perceive the hazard, misjudged the risk, or misaligned the coordinates. To address this opacity, we investigate whether decomposing a single VLM into distinct, inspectable phases incorporating scene captioning, structured OSHA-aligned hazard reasoning, visual grounding, multi-modal verification, and conflict reconciliation can improve detection accuracy, localization precision, and error traceability. We introduce GROVE (Grounded Violation Evaluator), a pipeline where a single Qwen 3.5 9B backbone executes all textual reasoning phases, paired with a grounding module and a specialized fallback mechanism to localize abstract or relational hazards. We evaluated GROVE against eleven single-pass baselines using a real-world construction image benchmark. While the detection scores of single-pass models cluster near 0.55, GROVE achieves an identification F1 of 0.745. In addition, GROVE significantly enhances localization, achieving a ground-truth coverage score of 0.802 compared to just 0.389 for the baseline Qwen model deployed in a single-pass configuration. GROVE also ensures near-complete visual accountability by generating bounding boxes for 99.7% of flagged hazards, reducing the missing-box rate to just 0.3% relative to the 11.1% to 73.6% rates observed in baseline models. Crucially, this modular architecture allows us to trace the majority of identification errors back to the hazard-reasoning stage. These results are established on a construction safety dataset, and demonstrate a single domain use case. GROVE is therefore best positioned as a transparent, auditable decision-support tool for offline inspection and its per-stage design offers a path toward a reliable, and transparent AI deployment.

title: The Chartered Agent: How the City of London Wrote the Legal Architecture of Agentic Commerce in 1555
abstract: The rise of autonomous AI shopping and procurement agents has reopened the debate on legal personhood for artificial intelligence. This paper argues that, for the domain of commerce, the debate is asking the wrong question. Commerce does not need AI persons; it needs chartered agents, and the legal template already exists. The paper traces a single institutional arc: from the civic autonomy of the City of London (1067, 1215), through the guild system's invention of collective non-human legal actors, to the chartering of the Muscovy Company on February 6, 1555, the first major English chartered joint-stock company. That charter solved a problem structurally identical to the one autonomous AI agents pose today: how to let a non-human construct transact, hold assets, bear obligations, and absorb liability on behalf of human principals operating in an environment that exceeds individual human capacity. The paper then connects this history to the Shopper Schism, the author's framework describing the structural disaggregation of the consumer function (retained by humans) from the shopper function (delegated to algorithms), and shows that the joint-stock company and the algorithmic shopper are two instances of the same 1 delegation architecture, applied first to the supply side of commerce and now, for the first time, to the demand side. Reviewing the legal record, including Thaler v Comptroller-General [2023] UKSC 49, Moffatt v Air Canada (2024), the CFTC enforcement action against Ooki DAO, and the Wyoming, Tennessee, and Utah DAO statutes, the paper shows that courts consistently refuse to invent AI personhood while legislatures are quietly converging on the chartered-company architecture. It concludes with a Chartered Agent framework for agentic commerce and a research agenda spanning management, law, and economic history.

title: Reimagining Assessment of Learning in the Era of Generative Artificial Intelligence
abstract: Introduction-The rapid integration of generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) renders traditional, artifact-based higher education assessments obsolete. This study explores how institutions must transition toward evaluating the holistic learning process rather than static end products. Methodology-A Narrative Literature Review was employed to synthesize interdisciplinary evidence, encompassing quantitative AI adoption trends, qualitative cognitive judgment evaluations, and multidimensional institutional readiness frameworks. Results-Findings indicate that the GenAI disruption constitutes a "wicked problem" requiring a shift to a Postplagiarism Framework, where human-AI co-authorship is standard practice. Traditional assessment formats are highly vulnerable, necessitating structural redesigns such as interactive oral vivas and process portfolio audits. Furthermore, standardized models like the AI Assessment Scale (AIAS) and the GENESIS framework are crucial for establishing explicit permissibility levels and executing systemic programmatic reform. Discussion-Higher education must abandon punitive detection strategies in favor of cultivating students' evaluative judgment and critical AI literacy. Implementing resilient, process-driven evaluations combined with synchronous checkpoints is essential to secure the validity of educational credentialing in an AI-saturated learning landscape.

title: Can the "Answer Independence" Claim in Conversational AI Advertising Be Verified? Construct Decomposition and the Derivation of Audit Requirements
abstract: When OpenAI introduced advertising into ChatGPT in 2026, it declared "Answer independence"-ads do not influence answers-as an official principle. This study takes that claim itself as the object of analysis, asking what must be measured to verify it and whether external observers can do so. Drawing on construct validity in measurement theory, we decompose "answer independence" into five sub-constructs-content invariance (C1), mention neutrality (C2), tone neutrality (C3), omission neutrality (C4), and longitudinal stability (C5)-and assess the verification conditions and external verifiability of each, building on the algorithm auditing literature. Verifiability proves asymmetric across constructs: C1 and C3 are partially verifiable, C2 is verifiable only conditional on sponsor disclosure, C4 is unverifiable in principle absent a stipulated completeness criterion, and C5 is practically unverifiable without access to training pipelines. We further observe a mismatch between the scope of the claim and the scope of its grounds: while the claim itself is broad enough to cover all five constructs, the grounds offered for it (system separation and labeling) address only the easily verifiable layer. From this matrix we derive three audit requirements-sponsor disclosure, counterfactual access, and third-party pipeline auditing-and connect them to existing institutional precedents including advertising substantiation doctrines in U.S. and Korean law, third-party measurement accreditation, and researcher data access under the EU DSA. As, to our knowledge, the first framework to decompose a platform's independence claim through a measurement-theoretic lens and systematically grade its external verifiability, this study argues that the debate over conversational AI advertising should turn not on the truth of the claim but on its verifiability.

title: <p>Text And Data Mining Exceptions in the Generative AI Era: Preserving European Innovation and Competitiveness</p>
abstract: The rapid ascension of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) has sparked intense debate surrounding copyright frameworks and upstream data ingestion. This paper examines the critical role of Text and Data Mining (TDM) exceptions, specifically under Articles 3 and 4 of the EU Copyright in the Digital Single Market (CDSM) Directive, as the foundational infrastructure for European AI development. By analyzing historical technological shifts, recent European legislative implementations (including the Italian Copyright Law updates and the EU AI Act), and comparative international models like Japan's "non-enjoyment" framework, this brief argues against protectionist rollbacks. Restricting TDM exceptions introduces severe market consolidation risks, drives innovation out of the European Union, and exposes consumers to localized algorithmic biases from foreign-trained models. Instead, this paper advocates for a balanced approach that pairs robust TDM exceptions with standardized machine-readable opt-outs and statutory monetization mechanisms at the commercialization phase.&nbsp;

title: Synthetic Consumer Panels Without Microdata: Iterative Proportional Fitting from Open Aggregate Statistics
abstract: Large language model (LLM)-based synthetic survey respondents require a demographically representative population to condition on. Existing work assumes individual-level census microdata such as the US Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS), which in most of the world is gated, licensed, or non-existent. We show that a nationally representative synthetic consumer panel can be built from only freely published aggregate cross-tabulations via iterative proportional fitting (IPF), with no microdata seed. We instantiate it for Switzerland (no open PUMS) from two free Federal Statistical Office cubes, producing 10,000 personas whose marginals reproduce the population register (foreign-national share 27.9% vs. ≈26-27%). Using Semantic Similarity Rating (SSR; Maier et al., 2025) as the elicitation layer, the panel reproduces that method's two relative findings on our own stack: SSR yields graded distributions that Direct-Likert rating collapses (r = 0.82), and purchase intent shifts with income as expected. Because no consumer purchase-intent human ground truth is available to us, we validate the elicitation layer against real Swiss human ratings-the ENERGYSCAPE panel (35 landscape-technology concepts, ∼844 raters), cloning personas from its own demographics-where synthetic concept means track the human ranking at R xy = 0.68 against a near-saturated ceiling (R xx = 0.99, so ρ ≈ R xy). This is a cross-domain reference point: our task is landscape fit, whereas the ≈90% of Maier et al. is for fast-moving consumer goods (FMCG) purchase intent, and the IPF panel itself is validated only on marginal recovery. Demographic conditioning proves construct-dependent: it moves income-driven purchase intent but is flat for aesthetic landscape-fit (∆R xy ≈ 0); a secondary vaccine-acceptance check (n = 1,971) gives R xy = 0.55. The pipeline runs on public cloud for a few dollars, with a zero-cost offline embedder. SSR is the contribution of Maier et al.; ours is the open-data panel and its validation, with our own prompts and anchor statements (unpublished by Maier et al.) for full reproducibility.&nbsp;

title: The Möbius Vulnerability: The genealogy of LLM: Pathologies Epistemic Residue, Derivability Decay, and Temporal Failure in Generative AI Systems
abstract: This paper proposes a preliminary theory of epistemic pathologies in generative AI systems. Its central claim is that important failures cannot be understood only as hallucinations, biases, alignment defects or isolated errors. They must also be understood as temporal and genealogical phenomena: effects produced by the accumulation of epistemic residues along stochastic conversational trajectories. The paper introduces derivability as the capacity to reconstruct the path connecting input, transformation and output. It then defines the Möbius Vulnerability as the limit-event in which a model appears to correct itself while preserving the structural form of the error that generated the correction. Against evaluation paradigms that privilege isolated outputs, the paper argues for trajectory-based epistemic analysis. A model may remain locally coherent while becoming globally non-derivable. This condition gives rise to a deeper risk: the formation of plausible but weakly derivable epistemic environments, here called Mythos. The paper deliberately does not provide an implementation protocol, scoring system or operational architecture. It identifies the theoretical conditions under which derivability decay, correction failure and Möbius-like trajectories may emerge.

title: Serious Dialog Systems in an Age of LLMs: Rethinking Architecture, but Not Design
abstract: Technology underlying dialog-based applications has greatly advanced over the past quarter centuryfor capturing users' natural language responses and their sentiment, generating realistic characters and scenes, modeling of knowledge, and rendering using increasingly capable graphics. However, though architectural elements are greatly influenced by emerging technologies, design patterns remain largely unchanged-and appropriately so. This paper delves into reasons why design for these 'serious' applications is critical, and presents lessons learned that are relevant with new and exciting capabilities.

title: Technological Opportunity Identification via Multidimensional Technology Landscape: From Technology Element Expansion to Solution Generating
abstract: Technological innovation is recognized as a primary driver of manufacturing competitiveness. Among various information sources, patent documents contain rich and structured technical information and have therefore become a key basis for technological opportunity identification (TOI). However, the increasing complexity of manufacturing systems and the demand for production flexibility have exposed the limitations of the previous TOI methods, especially their limited semantic precision and technological homogeneity, which restricts their practical effectiveness and lead to innovation myopia. To address these issues, a method that integrates BERTopic topic modelling with the F-term classification system is proposed in this paper to achieve technology topic migration and technology element expansion. This approach enhances the granularity of technology elements and the depth of semantic analyses while introducing diverse technical concepts through cross-domain migration to facilitate technology fusion and reveal new technological opportunities. The approach consists of four phases: 1) collecting data from Derwent; 2) extracting technology topics on the basis of the BERTopic model; 3) screening using LLMs and NER based on the technical elements of the F system expansion.; and 4) constructing a multidimensional technology landscape and identifying technological opportunities. In this paper, the effectiveness of the proposed method is demonstrated by applying it to automatic aircraft riveting technology. The results indicate that the identified technological opportunities exhibit high granularity and strong practical applicability, facilitating the iterative development of automatic riveting processes and fostering technological innovations within aerospace manufacturing enterprises.

title: Tri-path DINO: Feature Complementary Learning for Remote Sensing Multi-Class Change Detection
abstract: In remote sensing imagery, multi-class change detection (MCD) is crucial for fine-grained monitoring,yet it has long been constrained by complex scene variations and the scarcity of detailed annotations. To address this, we propose the Tri-path DINO architecture, which adopts a three-path complementary feature learning strategy to facilitate the rapid adaptation of pre-trained foundation models to complex vertical domains. Specifically, we employ the DINOv3 pre-trained model as the backbone feature extraction network to learn coarse-grained features. An auxiliary path also adopts a siamese structure, progressively aggregating intermediate features from the siamese encoder to enhance the learningof fine-grained features. Finally, a multi-scale attention mechanism is introduced to augment the decoder network, where parallel convolutions adaptively capture and enhance contextual information under different receptive fields. The proposed method achieves optimal performance on the MCD task on both the Gaza facility damage assessment dataset (Gaza-change) and the classic SECOND dataset. Grad-CAM visualizations further confirm that the main and auxiliary paths naturally focus on coarse-grained semantic changes and fine-grained structural details, respectively. This synergistic complementarity provides a robust and interpretable solution for advanced change detection tasks, offering a basis for rapid and accurate damage assessment.

title: BreastAID: An Auditable Conflict-Aware Multimodal Reasoning Framework for Breast Cancer Decision Support
abstract: ·Background: Breast cancer diagnosis requires integration of heterogeneous evidence from imaging, pathology, and clinical records. In real-world workflows, these evidence sources may be incomplete or mutually inconsistent. Existing AI-based decision-support systems often lack explicit conflict management and auditable reasoning mechanisms.·Objective: This study aimed to develop and retrospectively evaluate the technical feasibility of an auditable multimodal reasoning framework for breast cancer decision support under incomplete and conflicting clinical evidence.·Methods: BreastAID integrates modality-specific perception modules, a central evidence memory, symbolic guideline constraints, and a constrained LLM-assisted arbitration module for structured conflict explanation and evidence summarization.·Results: In the controlled reasoning benchmark, BreastAID achieved a conflict-case accuracy of 0.921, an unsupported reasoning error rate of 0.100, and a conflict detection F1 score of 0.904. Across three retrospective institutional cohorts, BreastAID achieved diagnostic accuracies of 93.1%, 95.2%, and 89.4%, respectively. The framework demonstrated more stable reasoning behavior than conventional fusion baselines and unconstrained structured-evidence reasoning baselines under incomplete-modality and evidence-conflict conditions.·Conclusions: BreastAID demonstrates the technical feasibility of an auditable and conflict-aware multimodal clinical reasoning framework for breast cancer decision support. The findings suggest that explicit evidence-conflict management and audit-oriented reasoning may support more stable reasoning behavior under incomplete or discordant multimodal evidence conditions. However, the current findings should be interpreted as feasibility and reasoning-reliability evidence rather than definitive proof of clinical effectiveness. Further prospective patient-level multimodal validation and workflow-oriented clinical evaluation are required before real-world clinical deployment.

title: AI-Driven Energy Saving and Emission Reduction in Renewable Microgrids with Safe Learning and Quantum-Aware Optimization
abstract: Renewable-rich microgrids require fast, low-carbon scheduling that remains physically feasible under uncertain forecasts, market signals and operating events. This study develops an artificial intelligence-driven energy management framework that integrates a fine-tuned large language model, safety-projected deep reinforcement learning, and distributed quantum-aware optimization. The language model converts weather alerts, carbon price notices, maintenance logs, and operator rules into bound semantic risk features. A constrained actor–critic policy produces continuous setpoints for the generator, storage, grid, reserve, and demand response, whereas a projection layer enforces power balance, state-of-charge, ramping, reserve, exchange, and curtailment limits prior to implementation. Binary commitment and operating-mode decisions are then refined using a circuit-aware quantum approximate optimization model that penalizes gate count, inter-processor communication, circuit depth, and expected error. Three reproducible synthetic benchmark cases (campus, industrial, and islanded microgrids) were evaluated against eight representative methods using 30 paired stochastic trials. The proposed method achieved an average operating cost of 998.1 ± 28.5 US$, reducing the cost by 6.84% relative to the quantum-assisted model predictive control and by 7.71–25.69% relative to seven non-quantum baselines. It also reduced carbon emissions by 5.51–24.90%, renewable curtailment by 14.60–51.38%, loss-of-supply probability by 25.61–68.10%, state-of-charge violations by 28.75–75.76%, and risk by 12.86–45.73%. The results show that semantic intelligence, safety projection, and circuit-aware quantum refinement can jointly improve energy savings, emission reduction, reliability, and auditability.

title: The Anticipatory Economy and Ambient Commerce&nbsp; <div> AI, Geospatial Intelligence, and the Architecture of Presence-Triggered Trade </div>
abstract: A new coordination logic is forming across retail, logistics, and consumer services: economic activity organized around predicted demand rather than confirmed orders. This paper names and traces that logic as the anticipatory economy and introduces ambient commerce as its consumerfacing expression. In ambient commerce, the act of signalling intent to go somewhere, entering a destination into a navigation application, triggers the entire service chain ahead of arrival. A grocery store sees the customer coming, confirms inventory, stages the order, and reserves a curbside slot before the car leaves the driveway. A restaurant receives arrival time, requests a menu decision, and has the order placed before the customer walks in. Every transaction is just in time. Every service is pre-positioned. The physical visit becomes the final step in a transaction already substantially complete. We trace the verified evidence base for this emerging system. Walmart's hurricane-stocking program, running continuously since 2004, established that demand correlation can drive supply decisions without causal explanation. The more consequential development is the agentic commerce infrastructure built between September 2025 and early 2026, when OpenAI, Google, Microsoft, Stripe, PayPal, Visa, and Mastercard (Mastercard, 2026) launched competing transaction protocols allowing AI agents to discover, negotiate, and pay on a consumer's behalf in real time. McKinsey projects this channel will redirect 3 to 5 trillion dollars in global retail spending by 2030 (Roberts, 2025). We connect this evidence to classical economic theory (Hayek, Coase, North, Granovetter) and examine the implications for competition policy, data governance, and the participation of developing economies in standards-setting processes now underway.

title: The Darkest Mirror: How AI Authority Bias Threatens Individual Agency and What Architecture Must Do About It
abstract: The dominant safety framework for artificial intelligence assumes that humans, properly positioned in the decision chain, will catch what AI gets wrong. This paper argues the assumption is insufficient. The failure mode is authority bias: a documented feature of human cognitive architecture that AI deployment systematically triggers. Sustained critical oversight of accepted authority sources is unsustainable even for trained critical thinkers operating in good faith.<br><br>The mechanism is not new. Arendt named it as thoughtlessness. Milgram demonstrated it experimentally. Rand identified it as failed identity formation. Peterson traced it to how humans build maps of meaning. Forty years of cognitive science — Kahneman, Bainbridge, Parasuraman, Cialdini — converges on the same structural claim. Recent empirical work (Buçinca, Vicente and Matute, Shen and Tamkin, Sharma, the April 2026 Claude Mythos Preview System Card) documents it in AI-mediated decision-making at scale.<br><br>AI authority bias corrupts Boyd's OODA loop at the Orientation phase — the irreducibly human phase Boyd identified as the center of adaptive decision-making under uncertainty. The fix must live in the model, not in the humans downstream. The paper proposes four training-level requirements — calibrated uncertainty, explicit context flagging, competing interpretations, override-rewarding training — implementable through three governance channels: regulatory mandate, procurement requirements, and industry standard-setting.

title: Beyond Passive Learning: How genAI Triggers Recursive Self-Regulation
abstract: Existing literature on generative AI (genAI) in business education has examined students’ attitudes, motivation, and the ethics of genAI use, alongside ongoing debate about whether genAI over-reliance may diminish self-regulated learning (SRL).
